front: Theorist: wrote the first post-classical treatise on painting (1435); first to suggest artists use a vanishing point for illusionistic perspective
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: concept of istoria — argues that depicting narrative that interacts with the audience is the highest achievement of painting
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: developed a method for linear perspective later dubbed the 'legitimate construction'
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: second book opens by retelling the story of Narcissus, comparing painting to 'embracing the surface of the pool'
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: a challenge posed in this treatise inspired Renaissance artists to paint versions of The Calumny of Apelles
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: praises Botticelli's Adoration of the Magi for including a self-portrait that exemplifies istoria
back: Della Pittura / De Pictura (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Theorist: ten-book treatise on architecture updating Vitruvius's De Architectura
work: De Re Aedificatoria (Ten Books on Architecture)

front: Theorist: proclaims columns the most noble building element
work: De Re Aedificatoria (Ten Books on Architecture)

front: Building: Alberti completed the facade of this Florence church; features scrolls linking the upper and lower registers

back: Santa Maria Novella facade (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Building: polychrome marble facade; commissioned by wool merchant Giovanni Rucellai; scroll buttresses later copied by Giacomo della Porta for Il Gesù
Building: lower level has niches flanked by flat pilasters; upper level has an oriel window with paired pilasters
Building: remade a Franciscan church in Rimini; may have been the first pagan temple built in Europe since the Classical era
Building: entrance has three equal arches, two designed to hold sarcophagi of patron Sigismondo Malatesta and his wife Ixotta
Building: contains a chapel of the planets; proclaimed 'full of pagan gods and profane things'; patron was target of a 1461 crusade by Pope Pius II
Building: church in Mantua with a deep-arched entrance inside a temple front beneath another arch; colossal and Corinthian columns on facade
Building: Alberti's original design lacks a transept, dome, and choir
Building: although Alberti proclaimed columns the noblest element, they are completely absent from his facade of San Sebastiano (also in Mantua)
Building: built for the wool merchant Giovanni Rucellai; the exterior and loggia across the street were decorated with patron's trademark sail-blowing-in-wind symbol
back: Palazzo Rucellai and Rucellai Chapel (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Building: 30 marble squares are inlaid in a funerary chapel in San Pancrazio, Florence
back: Palazzo Rucellai and Rucellai Chapel (Leon Battista Alberti)

front: Building: unfinished bell tower in Ferrara made of alternating pink and white marble bands; associated with the Este school led by Mantegna
Architect: articulated the ideal of the Renaissance man with 'a man can do all things if he will'
Architect: wrote Philodoxius, a satirical Latin comedy still thought to be an ancient work 100 years after his death
Architect: invented the first polyalphabetic cipher, described in his work De Cifris
Architect: sometimes credited with writing the Hypnerotomachia Poliphili or making its woodcut illustrations
